_provenance.note: Discovery loop (Epic Y): the conjectures that graphstore.evaluate judged 'uncertain' (genuinely open, neither known nor false) refined into precise research directions by an IN-SESSION Claude Code sub-agent acting as an ML-theory research advisor. No API call (Max-plan in-session sub-agent), per the project methodology. Produced once in-session and committed; graphstore.discover validates this artifact DETERMINISTICALLY (schema + that every direction is anchored to a real conjecture the evaluation flagged uncertain). Closes the loop discover -> predict -> evaluate -> investigate. Extended in Epic AF with C5 and C7: the uncertain-judged conjectures from the fifth literature (online learning), refined by the same in-session sub-agent discipline and gated identically (anchoring + completeness over the open set).

directions.precise_statement: In Markov-chain/MDP settings, the contraction modulus of a Markovian operator is controlled by a likelihood-ratio/change-of-measure quantity: Dobrushin's ergodic coefficient and Doeblin's minorization condition (P(x,.) >= beta*nu(.)) yield geometric contraction with modulus (1-beta) in total variation, and in approximate dynamic programming the L2(mu)-contraction modulus of the projected Bellman operator is governed by concentrability coefficients C = sup ||d(P^pi rho)/d mu||_inf, i.e. a sup of Radon-Nikodym derivatives between the visitation distribution and the sampling distribution mu.
directions.whats_established: Doeblin's minorization gives geometric ergodicity with rate 1-beta (Meyn & Tweedie, Markov Chains and Stochastic Stability, Thm 16.0.2). Dobrushin's coefficient delta(P)=sup_{x,y}||P(x,.)-P(y,.)||_TV is submultiplicative and bounds TV-contraction. In ADP, Munos (2003, 'Error Bounds for Approximate Policy Iteration') and Munos & Szepesvari (2008, JMLR, 'Finite-Time Bounds for Fitted Value Iteration') show approximation-error propagation is governed by concentrability coefficients that are sup-norm Radon-Nikodym derivatives; Antos, Szepesvari & Munos (2008) extend this to single-sample-path fitted-Q. Bertsekas & Tsitsiklis (NDP, 1996) give the weighted-norm contraction of T^pi under the stationary-distribution-weighted L2 norm.
directions.open_question: Is there a sharp, non-trivial CHARACTERIZATION (not just an upper bound) of the L2(mu) contraction modulus of the projected Bellman operator Pi_mu T^pi in terms of a likelihood-ratio functional -- i.e. does the modulus equal (or is two-sided-bounded by) gamma times a measure of mismatch between mu and the discounted visitation distribution d^pi, rather than the loose sup-norm concentrability C that can be infinite even when the operator is contractive?
directions.next_step: Attempt to prove a two-sided bound gamma * f(mu, d^pi) <= ||Pi_mu T^pi||_{L2(mu) Lip} <= gamma * g(mu, d^pi), where f,g are explicit divergence-like functionals (e.g. chi-square divergence chi^2(d^pi||mu) or a spectral mismatch ratio lambda_max(Sigma_{d^pi}/Sigma_mu)) of the Radon-Nikodym derivative d(d^pi)/dmu; test tightness numerically on small finite MDPs (random chains, ~20 states) by comparing the empirically estimated operator Lipschitz modulus against the candidate functionals across a sweep of sampling distributions mu.

directions.precise_statement: The 'fixed point' core is type-correct not for the uniform-convergence statement itself but for the empirical-risk MINIMIZER: many M-estimators are defined by an estimating-equation/self-consistency fixed point, T_n(theta) = theta where T_n is the empirical map (e.g. the EM operator, the Newton/gradient map of a smooth empirical objective, or the proximal/contraction map of a regularized problem), and uniform convergence is what transfers properties of the population fixed point theta* (T(theta*)=theta*) to the empirical fixed point theta_n.
directions.whats_established: Self-consistency/fixed-point analyses of M-estimation are well developed: the EM operator's population fixed point and local geometric contraction are characterized by Balakrishnan, Wainwright & Yu (2017, Annals of Statistics, 'Statistical guarantees for the EM algorithm'); gradient-EM and general empirical-vs-population operator coupling appear there and in Wang, Gu, Ning & Liu (2015). Stochastic-approximation fixed-point theory (Robbins-Monro; Borkar, 'Stochastic Approximation: A Dynamical Systems Viewpoint') frames SGD/TD as tracking an ODE with an equilibrium. Uniform convergence (Vapnik-Chervonenkis; Bartlett-Mendelson Rademacher bounds, 2002) controls sup_f |R_n(f)-R(f)| but is itself a concentration statement, not a self-map.
directions.open_question: Can uniform-convergence rates be packaged as a quantitative perturbation bound on fixed points: given that the empirical operator T_n is uniformly close to the population operator T (sup ||T_n - T|| <= eps_n with high probability) and T is a local contraction with modulus kappa<1 near theta*, does ||theta_n - theta*|| <= eps_n/(1-kappa) hold with the SAME high probability, and what is the weakest uniform-convergence/stability condition on the operator class {T_theta} (as opposed to the loss class) that yields it?
directions.next_step: Prove a 'fixed-point uniform convergence' theorem: define the operator class F = {theta -> grad_theta ell(.,theta)} or the EM map, bound its Rademacher complexity / establish a uniform ||T_n - T||_inf <= eps_n bound, and combine with Banach perturbation (||theta_n-theta*|| <= eps_n/(1-kappa)) to get a finite-sample fixed-point error; instantiate concretely for Gaussian-mixture EM to recover the Balakrishnan-Wainwright-Yu rate purely from an operator-class uniform-convergence argument, then identify whether operator-class complexity can be strictly smaller than loss-class complexity.

directions.precise_statement: The type-correct core of C5 is a regime dichotomy for the limit objects of no-regret play. In multi-agent settings, the guaranteed limit object of no-regret dynamics is set-valued and statistical: the empirical distribution of joint play converges to the set of coarse correlated equilibria; there is in general no attracting fixed point of the iterates, and for bilinear zero-sum games the last iterate of vanilla OGD provably cycles or diverges (Poincare recurrence). The skolem fixed point becomes a real object exactly when the game operator F is mu-strongly monotone and L-Lipschitz: then for step size eta < 2*mu/L^2 the OGD update z -> z - eta*F(z) is a Banach contraction with modulus sqrt(1 - 2*eta*mu + eta^2*L^2) < 1, and the unique Nash equilibrium is its fixed point, attracting the last iterate geometrically. Between these poles (merely monotone operators, constrained sets, non-monotone games) the behavior of the vanilla last iterate is governed by the spectrum of the Jacobian I - eta*DF(z*) at equilibrium: eigenvalues inside the unit disk give local contraction, eigenvalues on the unit circle give rotation/recurrence. The sharpened claim is therefore: 'no-regret play has an attracting fixed point iff the linearized OGD map at equilibrium is a local contraction, with strong monotonicity as the global sufficient condition; otherwise the correct limit object is the CCE set of averaged play.' The research direction is to characterize the phase boundary between these regimes, especially under constraints and in non-monotone games, where neither pole applies cleanly.
directions.whats_established: The folk theorem that time-averaged no-regret play converges to the CCE set is classical (Hannan consistency; Hart & Mas-Colell; Cesa-Bianchi & Lugosi). Bailey & Piliouras (2018) and Mertikopoulos, Papadimitriou & Piliouras (2018) proved last-iterate cycling/recurrence of FTRL and OGD in zero-sum bilinear games. Strongly monotone variational inequality theory (Facchinei & Pang) gives the global contraction and unique fixed point, with last-iterate rates even under bandit feedback (Bravo, Leslie & Mertikopoulos 2018). Optimism repairs the merely-monotone case: extragradient and optimistic GDA have last-iterate convergence (Daskalakis et al. 2018; Golowich, Pattathil & Daskalakis 2020; Cai, Oikonomou & Zheng 2022 for constrained monotone rates). What is missing is a characterization for vanilla, non-optimistic OGD strictly between strong monotonicity and bilinear recurrence, particularly with constraint boundaries.
directions.open_question: For vanilla fixed-step projected OGD on a constrained game with a monotone-but-not-strongly-monotone (or locally non-monotone) operator, characterize exactly - in terms of the spectrum of the projected linearization at equilibrium and the geometry of the active constraint face - when the last iterate converges for all initializations versus enters recurrence. In particular: is spectral radius strictly less than 1 of the projected Jacobian I - eta*DF(z*) restricted to the tangent cone both necessary and sufficient for local last-iterate convergence, and does constraint projection ever convert a recurrent unconstrained dynamic into a convergent one at positive measure of initializations?
directions.next_step: A pure-numpy sweep on parametrized two-player quadratic games: operator F(z) = (mu*I + gamma*S)z + b with S skew-symmetric, sweeping mu in [0, 1] (strong-monotonicity dial) and gamma (rotation dial), plus box constraints toggled on/off. For each (mu, gamma, eta) cell, run vanilla projected OGD from random initializations, record last-iterate distance to equilibrium and averaged-play distance to the CCE set, and separately compute the spectral radius of the (projected) linearized map. Plot the empirical convergence/cycling phase diagram against the predicted spectral-radius-equals-1 curve; the interesting cells are constrained ones where the active face changes the effective Jacobian. This directly mirrors the C2 finite-MDP sweep in scale: a few hundred lines, minutes of compute, and it either confirms or breaks the necessity direction of the conjectured spectral characterization.

directions.precise_statement: The type-correct core of C7 is that a single curvature modulus mu (equivalently condition number kappa = L/mu) simultaneously drives two theorems of different type, connected by a lossy bridge. Online: OGD with step 1/(mu*t) on any adversarial sequence of mu-strongly-convex losses has regret at most (G^2/(2*mu))(1 + log T) (Hazan-Agarwal-Kale), and this log T is tight. Offline: the fixed map x -> x - eta*grad f(x) on a single mu-strongly-convex, L-smooth f with eta <= 1/L is a (1 - mu*eta)-contraction, giving geometric last-iterate convergence. Online-to-batch conversion transports the first to the second but only up to O(log T / T) suboptimality of the averaged iterate - exponentially weaker than the contraction rate - so the analogy engine's CAUSE arrow is correct about the shared modulus but wrong to suggest the regimes are equivalent: adversarial regret provably cannot certify geometric convergence. The sharpened program is to quantify the interpolation between the two poles: for loss sequences that are all mu-strongly convex with minimizers drifting with path length P_T, the last iterate of constant-step OGD tracks at a rate 'geometric contraction plus kappa-dependent drift term,' recovering linear convergence when P_T = 0 and dynamic-regret behavior when P_T grows. The open frontier is whether one parameter-free algorithm can sit at both poles simultaneously without knowing which regime it is in.
directions.whats_established: Hazan, Agarwal & Kale (2007) established O(log T) regret for strongly convex OCO, with matching lower bounds (Takimoto & Warmuth; Abernethy et al. 2008). Online-to-batch conversion is Cesa-Bianchi, Conconi & Gentile (2004), with anytime variants by Cutkosky (2019); Rakhlin, Shamir & Sridharan (2012) showed suffix averaging attains O(1/T) for strongly convex SGD. The tracking/dynamic-regret pole is Mokhtari, Shahrampour, Jadbabaie & Ribeiro (2016): contraction-based dynamic regret O(P_T) for strongly convex online losses. Adaptivity to unknown curvature exists at the regret level - MetaGrad (van Erven & Koolen 2016) gets O(log T) on strongly convex sequences without knowing mu - but that machinery certifies averaged-iterate regret, not last-iterate geometric contraction. The offline contraction itself is textbook (Polyak; Nesterov).
directions.open_question: Does there exist a single parameter-free online algorithm that, on any adversarial sequence of mu-strongly-convex losses (mu unknown), guarantees O((G^2/mu) log T) regret, and additionally, whenever the sequence is eventually a fixed smooth strongly convex f, has last iterate converging geometrically at a rate polynomial in the true kappa - with a matching lower bound showing what degradation of the geometric rate (if any) is the necessary price of adversarial robustness? The question is genuinely open because existing curvature-adaptive methods (MetaGrad-style) control averaged regret only, and known last-iterate geometric guarantees all require knowing step sizes tied to mu and L in advance.
directions.next_step: A numpy experiment on quadratics f_t(x) = 0.5*(x - c_t)' H (x - c_t) with H of condition number kappa in {1, 10, 100}: three regimes - fixed c_t (offline pole), drifting c_t with controlled path length P_T, and adversarially alternating c_t. Run (a) OGD with eta_t = 1/(mu*t), (b) constant-step OGD with eta = 1/L, and (c) a simple curvature-adaptive candidate (e.g., a doubling-trick hedge over a geometric grid of step sizes, or coin-betting), logging both cumulative regret and last-iterate error. Verify the predicted log T / T versus geometric versus contraction-plus-P_T rates, then measure whether the adaptive candidate's last-iterate rate on the fixed-loss regime degrades with kappa faster than the informed constant-step baseline - that degradation curve is direct evidence for or against a price-of-robustness lower bound. This is the same scale as the C4 Gaussian-mixture-EM experiments: small, deterministic, and it turns the open question's quantitative form into a measurable exponent.
